none: Average fuel economy standard for nonpassenger automobiles, model year 1979 : environmental impact statement.

The National Highway Traffic Safety Administration (NHTSA) proposed new fuel economy standards for nonpassenger vehicles like vans and pickup trucks for the 1979 model year. The goal was to improve fuel efficiency to conserve energy, given the nation's reliance on petroleum and past gasoline shortages. The proposed standards aimed to balance environmental benefits with the economic impact on manufacturers, ensuring they could meet the standards without drastic changes. The standards were expected to save millions of gallons of gasoline annually, contributing to national energy conservation efforts.
Average fuel economy standard for nonpassenger automobiles, model year 1979 : environmental impact statement.

The National Highway Traffic Safety Administration (NHTSA) proposed new fuel economy standards for nonpassenger vehicles like vans and pickup trucks for the 1979 model year. The goal was to improve fuel efficiency to conserve energy, given the nation's reliance on petroleum and past gasoline shortages. The proposed standards aimed to balance environmental benefits with the economic impact on manufacturers, ensuring they could meet the standards without drastic changes. The standards were expected to save millions of gallons of gasoline annually, contributing to national energy conservation efforts.

The proposed fuel economy standards for nonpassenger automobiles in 1979 were a response to the energy crisis and the need to reduce dependence on oil. There was some public support for improving fuel efficiency, but manufacturers expressed concerns about the economic impact and feasibility of meeting the new standards. Ultimately, the standards were implemented, contributing to long-term changes in vehicle design and fuel efficiency.
United States. National Highway Traffic Safety Administration

"The decreasing world petroleum supply and the uncertain availability to this Nation of existing foreign petroleum, combined with the importance of petroleum to the national economy and standard of living have made these fuel economy standards necessary." — Supplementary Information Section

"The agency believes that Congress intended the Title to have broad application, and that any interpretation of the Title that would have the effect of exempting an entire class of vehicles from regulation under the Title must be firmly based in the language of the Title or its legislative history." — NHTSA's Interpretation of Congressional Intent
